labs.title: Subnet Calculation
labs.concept: Calculate network address, broadcast, and host range from CIDR notation
labs.description: The subnet mask determines how many bits are used for the network vs host portion. Subtract prefix from 32 to get host bits, then 2^(host bits) - 2 = usable hosts (minus network and broadcast addresses).
instructions: Given the network 192.168.10.0/26, calculate by hand: network address, broadcast, first host, last host, number of usable hosts
Verify your calculation matches the output
Formula: host bits = 32 - prefix, usable hosts = 2^(host bits) - 2
Now try with /20: calculate 10.0.0.0/20 — how many usable hosts?
Run: ipcalc 10.0.0.0/20
Compare: /26 gives 62 hosts, /20 gives 4094 hosts
labs.expectedOutput:
/26 → 62 usable hosts, network .0, broadcast .63
/20 → 4094 usable hosts
labs.explanation: The subnet mask determines how many bits are used for the network vs host portion. Subtract prefix from 32 to get host bits, then 2^(host bits) - 2 = usable hosts (minus network and broadcast addresses).

labs.title: Binary to Decimal Conversion
labs.concept: Convert between binary and decimal — essential for understanding IP addresses and subnet masks
labs.description: Binary uses base 2. Each position represents a power of 2 (128, 64, 32, 16, 8, 4, 2, 1). An octet (8 bits) can represent values 0-255. Understanding binary is key to reading subnet masks.
instructions: Convert 200 to binary by hand: does 128 fit? (yes→1) 200-128=72. Does 64 fit? (yes→1) 72-64=8. Does 32 fit? (no→0). Does 16 fit? (no→0). Does 8 fit? (yes→1). 4? (no→0). 2? (no→0). 1? (no→0). Result: 11001000
Run: echo "obase=2;200" | bc
Verify your answer matches
Now convert binary 11000000 to decimal: 128+64 = 192
Run: echo "ibase=2;11000000" | bc
Practice: What is 255 in binary? What is 11111111 in decimal?
labs.explanation: Binary uses base 2. Each position represents a power of 2 (128, 64, 32, 16, 8, 4, 2, 1). An octet (8 bits) can represent values 0-255. Understanding binary is key to reading subnet masks.

labs.title: Network Interface Inspection
labs.concept: View and understand network interfaces, IP addresses, and link state on a Linux machine
labs.description: Every Linux machine has at least a loopback interface (127.0.0.1). Physical or virtual interfaces get IP addresses from DHCP or static config. The prefix shows the subnet size.
instructions: Run: ip a
Identify the loopback interface (lo) — what IP does it have?
Find your main network interface (eth0, ens*, or enp*)
Note the IPv4 address and its prefix length (e.g., /24)
Note the MAC address (link/ether line)
Run: ip link show — observe which interfaces are UP vs DOWN
Run: ifconfig — compare the output format to ip a
labs.explanation: Every Linux machine has at least a loopback interface (127.0.0.1). Physical or virtual interfaces get IP addresses from DHCP or static config. The prefix shows the subnet size.

labs.title: Routing Table Analysis
labs.concept: Inspect the routing table to understand how packets are forwarded to different networks
labs.description: The routing table tells the kernel where to send packets. The default route (0.0.0.0/0) handles all traffic without a more specific match. Directly connected subnets appear as kernel routes.
instructions: Run: ip route
Find the default route — what is the gateway IP?
Identify any directly connected networks (no "via" keyword)
Run: ip route get 8.8.8.8 — which interface and gateway does it use?
Look for link-local routes (169.254.0.0/16) — what are they for?
Note: the default route is where all traffic goes if no specific route matches
labs.expectedOutput:
default via 10.0.0.1 dev eth0
10.0.0.0/24 dev eth0 proto kernel
labs.explanation: The routing table tells the kernel where to send packets. The default route (0.0.0.0/0) handles all traffic without a more specific match. Directly connected subnets appear as kernel routes.

labs.title: Network and Broadcast Addresses
labs.concept: Understand the special role of network address (all host bits = 0) and broadcast address (all host bits = 1)
labs.description: Network address = lowest IP in a subnet (all host bits 0). Broadcast = highest IP (all host bits 1). These two are reserved and cannot be assigned to hosts, which is why usable hosts = 2^(host bits) - 2.
instructions: Identify: network address = .0, broadcast = .255
Now the broadcast is .63 (not .255!) — why?
Calculate: /26 means 6 host bits → block size = 64 → first block is .0-.63
The network address has all host bits set to 0
The broadcast address has all host bits set to 1
Neither can be assigned to a device — that is why we subtract 2 from usable hosts
labs.expectedOutput:
/24: network=.0, broadcast=.255, hosts=254
/26: network=.0, broadcast=.63, hosts=62
labs.explanation: Network address = lowest IP in a subnet (all host bits 0). Broadcast = highest IP (all host bits 1). These two are reserved and cannot be assigned to hosts, which is why usable hosts = 2^(host bits) - 2.

labs.title: Cloud VM Setup Basics
labs.concept: Understand the steps to create a virtual machine in a cloud environment (Azure/Hetzner)
labs.description: Cloud VMs are accessed via SSH using the public IP. Always update packages first. Security groups control inbound/outbound traffic (port 22 for SSH must be open). The VM costs money while running.
instructions: When creating a cloud VM, you need: a subscription, a resource group, and a virtual network
After creation, note the public IP address assigned to the VM
Connect via SSH: ssh user@<public-ip>
Check your user groups: id (look for group 27 = sudo)
First thing on any new VM: sudo apt update && sudo apt upgrade -y
If libc gets updated, reboot the machine: sudo reboot
Reconnect and verify the system is up to date
commands: sudo apt update && sudo apt upgrade -y
labs.explanation: Cloud VMs are accessed via SSH using the public IP. Always update packages first. Security groups control inbound/outbound traffic (port 22 for SSH must be open). The VM costs money while running.

labs.title: Cloud Networking Concepts
labs.concept: Virtual networks, security groups, load balancers, and bastion hosts in cloud environments
labs.description: Cloud providers create virtual networks with private IPs. Public IPs are mapped via NAT. Never expose a web server directly — use a reverse proxy, load balancer, or bastion host in front.
instructions: On a cloud VM, run: ip a — observe the private IP (e.g., 10.0.0.4)
Run: ip route — find the default gateway for the virtual network
The VM has a private IP internally but a public IP for external access
Security groups act as a firewall: inbound rules control what traffic reaches the VM
A bastion host is a gateway that accepts all incoming connections and forwards them securely inside
A load balancer distributes traffic across multiple VMs for high availability
Availability zones are separate fire compartments in a data center with independent power and cooling
Draw a diagram: Internet → Load Balancer → Bastion Host → Internal VMs
labs.expectedOutput: Private IP from virtual network, default route via cloud gateway
labs.explanation: Cloud providers create virtual networks with private IPs. Public IPs are mapped via NAT. Never expose a web server directly — use a reverse proxy, load balancer, or bastion host in front.

labs.title: Link-Local Addresses (169.254.x.x)
labs.concept: Understand link-local addressing — automatic IPs assigned when DHCP is unavailable
labs.description: Link-local (APIPA) addresses are a fallback when DHCP fails. If you see 169.254.x.x on your interface, it means the device could not reach a DHCP server. Fix the DHCP configuration or assign a static IP.
instructions: Run: ip a — look for any 169.254.x.x addresses
Run: ip route — check if there is a link-local route
Link-local addresses (169.254.0.0/16) are automatically assigned when no DHCP server responds
They only work for communication on the same local network segment
They cannot be routed to the internet
Question: If your machine gets a 169.254.x.x address, what is likely wrong?
labs.expectedOutput: Link-local route in routing table; possibly a 169.254.x.x address on an interface
labs.explanation: Link-local (APIPA) addresses are a fallback when DHCP fails. If you see 169.254.x.x on your interface, it means the device could not reach a DHCP server. Fix the DHCP configuration or assign a static IP.

labs.title: User Permissions and Sudo
labs.concept: Check user groups and sudo permissions on a Linux system
labs.description: The sudo group (group 27 on Debian/Ubuntu) grants a user permission to execute commands as root. Always use sudo instead of logging in as root directly — it provides an audit trail and limits damage from mistakes.
instructions: Run: id — note your uid, gid, and group memberships
Look for group 27 (sudo) — this means you can use sudo
Run: groups — shorter output of your group memberships
Run: sudo whoami — should print "root" if you have sudo rights
Run: cat /etc/group | grep sudo — see which users are in the sudo group
Note: on cloud VMs you typically log in as a regular user with sudo, not as root directly
labs.explanation: The sudo group (group 27 on Debian/Ubuntu) grants a user permission to execute commands as root. Always use sudo instead of logging in as root directly — it provides an audit trail and limits damage from mistakes.

labs.title: System Update Workflow
labs.concept: Update and upgrade packages on a new or existing Linux system
labs.description: Package updates fix security vulnerabilities and bugs. Always update a new VM immediately. A reboot is required when core libraries (libc) or the kernel are updated, since running processes still use the old version in memory.
instructions: First thing on any new VM: sudo apt update (refresh package lists)
Then: sudo apt upgrade -y (install all available updates)
Watch the output — note which packages get updated (ssh, libc, etc.)
If libc (the C library) is updated, you MUST reboot: sudo reboot
Why? Because libc is used by almost every process — a reboot ensures all processes use the new version
After reboot, reconnect via SSH and verify: apt list --upgradable
labs.expectedOutput:
Reading package lists... Done
0 upgraded, 0 newly installed after reboot
labs.explanation: Package updates fix security vulnerabilities and bugs. Always update a new VM immediately. A reboot is required when core libraries (libc) or the kernel are updated, since running processes still use the old version in memory.

labs.title: SSH Configuration
labs.concept: Understand SSH server configuration and how to handle config file changes during updates
labs.description: SSH is the standard way to access remote Linux servers securely. When packages update, new config versions may conflict with local changes. On cloud VMs, keep the local version since it is tuned for the cloud environment.
instructions: Connect to a remote machine: ssh user@<ip-address>
View the SSH server configuration: cat /etc/ssh/sshd_config
Check SSH service status: sudo systemctl status sshd
During updates, you may see a dialog asking about config differences
Options: keep local version (recommended if cloud-configured), install new version, or view the diff
Key settings to know: Port, PermitRootLogin, PasswordAuthentication, ClientAliveInterval
labs.explanation: SSH is the standard way to access remote Linux servers securely. When packages update, new config versions may conflict with local changes. On cloud VMs, keep the local version since it is tuned for the cloud environment.

labs.title: Ping and Tracepath
labs.concept: Test connectivity with ping and trace the network path with tracepath
labs.description: Ping tests basic connectivity using ICMP. Tracepath shows every router (hop) between you and the destination. High packet loss or no reply at a hop indicates a network issue at that point.
instructions: Run: ping -c 4 8.8.8.8 — sends 4 ICMP echo requests to Google DNS
Check: packets transmitted, received, and packet loss percentage
Note the round-trip time (min/avg/max)
Run: tracepath -m 15 8.8.8.8 — trace the path packets take
Count the hops — more hops generally means more latency
Look for high latency hops or "no reply" entries
Compare: ping a local host vs a remote host — notice the RTT difference
labs.expectedOutput:
4 packets transmitted, 4 received, 0% loss, avg ~20ms
Tracepath shows 5-15 hops depending on location
labs.explanation: Ping tests basic connectivity using ICMP. Tracepath shows every router (hop) between you and the destination. High packet loss or no reply at a hop indicates a network issue at that point.
